title: TaintFlow: Quantitative Information-Flow Auditing for ML Pipeline Leakage
one_line_summary: A hybrid dynamic-static ML pipeline auditor that executes once under lightweight instrumentation to extract a precise dataflow DAG, then applies provenance-parameterized channel capacity bounds — including provably tight bounds for common operations — to produce per-feature, per-stage leakage measurements in bits with formally conditional soundness guarantees.

mathematical_foundations.core_insight: Statistical operations in ML pipelines act as lossy information-theoretic channels whose capacity can be bounded using each operation's algebraic structure and the exact observed train-test mixing ratio (provenance). The data-processing inequality enables compositional reasoning: sequential stages compose via capacity addition, parallel branches via the chain rule.

definitions.name: Pipeline Information DAG (PI-DAG)
definitions.formal_statement: A PI-DAG is a directed acyclic graph G = (V, E, tau, rho, sigma) where V is a finite set of operation nodes, E ⊆ V × V × ColNames is the set of typed data-flow edges, tau: V → OpType assigns each node an operation type from a finite catalog, rho: V × ColNames → [0,1] assigns the observed test-row fraction per node per column, and sigma: V → (N × N × N) records (n_rows, n_cols, n_test_rows) shape metadata.
definitions.novelty: low — DAG representation is standard; the provenance annotation rho is the novel element

definitions.name: Partition-Taint Lattice
definitions.formal_statement: The partition-taint lattice is T = (P({tr, te, ext}) × B, ⊑) where B = {0, 1, 2, ..., B_max, ∞} with B_max = 64 (default). The partial order is (O₁, b₁) ⊑ (O₂, b₂) iff O₁ ⊆ O₂ ∧ b₁ ≤ b₂. Join: (O₁ ∪ O₂, max(b₁, b₂)). Meet: (O₁ ∩ O₂, min(b₁, b₂)). Bottom: (∅, 0). Top: ({tr,te,ext}, ∞). Height: |P({tr,te,ext})| has longest chain length 3 (∅ ⊂ {x} ⊂ {x,y} ⊂ {x,y,z}); B has chain length 66 (0 < 1 < ... < 64 < ∞). Product lattice height = 3 + 66 = 69.
definitions.novelty: moderate — product lattice is standard (Cousot & Cousot 1979); instantiation for DataFrame information flow with dual qualitative-quantitative tracking is new

definitions.name: DataFrame Abstract Domain
definitions.formal_statement: The DataFrame abstract domain is A_df = (ColNames →_fin T) × R where R ∈ {train-only, test-only, mixed(ρ)} with ρ ∈ [0,1] tracking the test-row fraction via roaring bitmaps. An abstract DataFrame state maps each column name to a lattice element (O, b) and carries row-provenance metadata. The abstraction relation α maps concrete (DataFrame, partition) pairs to abstract states: α(D, P) = (λj. (origins(D_j, P), 0), provenance(D, P)) for initial states, where origins computes which partition(s) contributed to column j.
definitions.novelty: moderate — extends standard column-wise abstract domains with provenance tracking

definitions.name: Channel Capacity Transfer Function
definitions.formal_statement: For operation type τ_k, the transfer function F_k: A_df → A_df maps input abstract state to output abstract state. For each output column j, F_k computes: (O_out, b_out) where O_out = ∪{O_i : column i influences column j through τ_k} and b_out = C_{τ_k}(ρ_k, n_k, d_k) + max{b_i : column i is a direct input to column j}, where C_{τ_k} is the channel capacity bound for operation type τ_k parameterized by observed provenance ρ_k, sample size n_k, and relevant dimensionality d_k. Transfer functions are monotone: σ₁ ⊑ σ₂ implies F_k(σ₁) ⊑ F_k(σ₂).
definitions.novelty: high — provenance-parameterized capacity bounds as abstract transfer functions are new

definitions.name: Sensitivity Function
definitions.formal_statement: A sensitivity function for operation k is δ_k: [0,∞]^{d_in} → [0,∞]^{d_out} satisfying: (1) Monotonicity: b ≤ b' componentwise implies δ_k(b) ≤ δ_k(b') componentwise; (2) DPI-consistency: for sequential composition, ||δ_{k+1} ∘ δ_k(b)||₁ ≤ ||δ_k(b)||₁ + C_{k+1} where C_{k+1} is the channel capacity of stage k+1; (3) Sub-additivity for independent inputs: δ_k(b + b') ≤ δ_k(b) + δ_k(b') when b, b' arise from independent sources.
definitions.novelty: moderate — Lipschitz-type conditions applied to information-flow; connection to DP composition is new

definitions.name: Fit-Transform Channel Model
definitions.formal_statement: For an estimator e with fit_transform(X) = transform(X; θ) where θ = fit(X), the operation defines two sub-channels: (1) The aggregation channel C_agg: X_te → θ, carrying at most C_fit(φ_e, ρ) bits from test rows to fitted parameters; (2) The application channel C_app: (X_j, θ) → Y_j, a pointwise transformation. When fit computes a sufficient statistic θ = T(X) such that the transform g_θ(x) depends on test data only through θ, the mutual information decomposes as I(D_te; Y_j) ≤ C_fit(φ_e, ρ) + b_{input,j}.
definitions.novelty: high — this channel model for the fit-transform feedback pattern is genuinely new in QIF

theorems.name: Fit-Transform Channel Decomposition Lemma
theorems.formal_statement: Let e be an estimator with fit operation computing sufficient statistic θ = T(X) and transform operation g_θ(x_j) depending on test data only through θ. Let X = X_tr ∪ X_te with |X_te|/|X| = ρ. Then for any output feature j: I(D_te; fit_transform(e, X)_j) ≤ C_fit(φ_e, ρ) + b_{input,j}, where C_fit(φ_e, ρ) is the channel capacity of the aggregation channel from test rows to fitted parameters, and b_{input,j} is the pre-existing leakage bound on input feature j.
theorems.proof_sketch: The proof proceeds in three steps. Step 1 (Markov factorization): Under the sufficient-statistic assumption, the chain D_te → (X, θ) → Y_j factors through D_te → θ and (X_j, θ) → Y_j. Specifically, for each test row x_te, its influence on Y_j = g_θ(X_j) passes through exactly two paths: (a) through θ via the fit operation, and (b) through x_te's own row in X_j via the transform operation. Step 2 (DPI application): By the data-processing inequality on the Markov chain D_te → θ → g_θ(X_j), the information through the fit path is bounded by I(D_te; θ) ≤ C_fit(φ_e, ρ). Step 3 (Additive composition): The total information I(D_te; Y_j) ≤ I(D_te; θ) + I(D_te; X_j | θ) by the chain rule of mutual information. The second term I(D_te; X_j | θ) ≤ b_{input,j} since conditioning on θ can only reduce or maintain the dependence of Y_j on D_te through X_j. The bound follows by I(D_te; Y_j) ≤ C_fit + b_{input,j}.
assumptions: A_pointwise: transform is pointwise (row-independent given θ)
excluded_estimators: KNNImputer (transform depends on all rows, not just θ)

theorems.name: Execution-Path-Conditional Soundness
theorems.formal_statement: Let G_obs be the PI-DAG extracted from executing pipeline π on dataset D with partition P = (D_tr, D_te). Let σ# be the abstract fixpoint computed by the worklist algorithm over G_obs using transfer functions {F_k} from the channel capacity catalog. Then, conditioned on: (C1) The instrumentation faithfully captures all pandas/sklearn API-level data-flow edges on the observed execution path; (C2) Channel capacity bounds C_{τ_k}(ρ_k, n_k, d_k) ≥ I(D_te; output_k | inputs_k) hold for each operation; (C3) The PI-DAG G_obs is acyclic or all cycles are handled by pattern-specific unrolling; we have: for all output features j, I(D_te; π(D)_j) ≤ σ#_j.b.
theorems.proof_sketch: By induction on the topological order of G_obs. Base case: source nodes (data loading) have σ#_j = α(D, P)_j with b = 0, which holds since the loaded data has zero leakage before any operation. Inductive step: For node v_k with predecessors {v_{k₁}, ..., v_{k_m}} already satisfying the bound, the transfer function F_k computes b_out = C_{τ_k}(ρ_k) + max{b_{input,i}}. By the chain rule of MI and DPI: I(D_te; output_j(v_k)) ≤ I(D_te; θ_k) + I(D_te; input_j | θ_k) ≤ C_{τ_k}(ρ_k) + b_{input,j} ≤ σ#_j.b. The key step for fit_transform nodes applies T1 (decomposition lemma). For pure transform nodes (no fit), C_{τ_k} = 0 and the bound reduces to b_out = b_input (DPI: processing cannot create information). For merge/join nodes, the chain rule handles multiple inputs.
assumptions: C3: DAG acyclicity or cycle handling

theorems.name: Sensitivity Function Composition
theorems.formal_statement: If δ₁, δ₂, ..., δ_K are sensitivity functions for pipeline stages 1 through K, each satisfying monotonicity, DPI-consistency, and sub-additivity, then: (a) Sequential composition: δ_π = δ_K ∘ ... ∘ δ₁ is a valid sensitivity function for the composed pipeline; (b) Parallel composition: for disjoint column subsets {S₁, ..., S_m}, the parallel composition δ_{||} defined by δ_{||}(b)_j = δ_i(b|_{S_i})_j for j ∈ S_i is a valid sensitivity function; (c) The composed sensitivity function satisfies ||δ_π(b)||₁ ≤ Σ_k C_k + ||b||₁ where C_k is the channel capacity of stage k.
theorems.proof_sketch: Part (a): Composition of monotone functions is monotone. DPI-consistency is preserved because each stage can add at most C_k bits, giving telescoping bound. Sub-additivity composes for independent inputs by linearity of the bound. Part (b): Parallel branches on disjoint columns are informationally independent, so the chain rule gives I(D_te; Y_{S₁}, ..., Y_{S_m}) = Σ_i I(D_te; Y_{S_i}). Part (c): Follows from telescoping the DPI-consistency condition across all K stages.

theorems.name: Information-Theoretic Min-Cut Attribution
theorems.formal_statement: For PI-DAG G_obs with edge capacities I_e from the abstract analysis, the leakage from D_te to any output feature j is bounded by: I(D_te; X_j^out) ≤ min_{S-T cut C} Σ_{e ∈ C} I_e, where S = {source nodes with test data} and T = {node producing feature j}. This bound is tight when all operations in the min-cut are injective (information-preserving).
theorems.proof_sketch: The information-theoretic max-flow/min-cut theorem (Cover & Thomas, Ch. 15) applied to the DAG with edge capacities equal to mutual information bounds. For deterministic injective operations f, I(X; f(X)) = H(f(X)) = H(X), so capacity equals flow. When non-injective operations appear in the cut, the bound may be strict. Multiple minimum cuts: resolve by reporting the one closest to the source (earliest pipeline stage) as the primary bottleneck.

theorems.name: Worklist Termination and Complexity
theorems.formal_statement: The worklist algorithm for computing the abstract fixpoint over PI-DAG G_obs with K nodes and d columns terminates in at most K × d × H iterations, where H = 69 is the lattice height. Each iteration performs O(1) lattice operations. Total time complexity: O(K × d × H) = O(K × d) for column-independent operations, and O(K × d²) for cross-column operations (PCA, covariance-based).
theorems.proof_sketch: By Tarski's fixed-point theorem, the least fixpoint exists since T is a complete lattice and all transfer functions are monotone. The worklist algorithm computes this fixpoint by iterating until no abstract state changes. Each iteration must increase at least one (node, column) lattice element strictly. Since the lattice height is finite (69), each element can increase at most 69 times. There are K × d elements total, giving the bound.

assumptions.statement: For estimators covered by T1, the fit operation computes a sufficient statistic θ = T(X) and the transform g_θ(x) depends on test data only through θ.
assumptions.plausibility: strong for StandardScaler/PCA/SimpleImputer; moderate for RobustScaler (median/IQR are not technically sufficient statistics — requires separate order-statistic argument); fails for KNNImputer/IterativeImputer
assumptions.what_breaks_if_fails: T1 decomposition lemma cannot be applied; affected estimators receive conservative ∞ bounds

assumptions.statement: Channel capacity bounds derived under the Gaussian channel model provide valid upper bounds for sub-Gaussian data distributions.
assumptions.plausibility: strong — Gaussian maximizes differential entropy for given variance (Shannon's theorem), so Gaussian channel capacity is an upper bound for sub-Gaussian inputs
assumptions.what_breaks_if_fails: Bounds may underestimate for super-Gaussian (heavy-tailed) data; mitigated by distribution-free fallback bounds

assumptions.statement: The Python instrumentation (sys.settrace + API monkey-patching) faithfully captures all pandas/sklearn API-level data-flow edges on the observed execution path.
assumptions.plausibility: moderate — covers standard API usage; may miss direct C-extension interactions bypassing Python API, internal Cython dispatch, or ctypes-based operations
assumptions.what_breaks_if_fails: Soundness condition C1 is violated; bounds may underestimate for missed operations. Mitigated by explicit enumeration of captured vs. uncaptured constructs.

assumptions.statement: The PI-DAG is acyclic, or all cycles correspond to recognized patterns (GridSearchCV, cross_val_score) that are handled by pattern-specific unrolling.
assumptions.plausibility: strong — ML pipelines are overwhelmingly feedforward; the ~40% with 'loops' are almost all CV wrappers with rigid structure
assumptions.what_breaks_if_fails: Worklist may not terminate without widening; mitigated by a simple widening operator (jump to ∞) for unrecognized cycles

assumptions.statement: For column-independent operations (StandardScaler, SimpleImputer, etc.), the transfer function processes each column independently.
assumptions.plausibility: strong — these operations are defined per-column by their APIs
assumptions.what_breaks_if_fails: The O(K×d) complexity bound increases to O(K×d²); the bounds themselves remain sound

assumptions.statement: The provenance fraction ρ at each node is exactly known from the dynamic instrumentation phase via roaring bitmap tracking.
assumptions.plausibility: strong for standard operations; moderate for operations that create new rows (resample, augmentation) where provenance of new rows is ambiguous
assumptions.what_breaks_if_fails: ρ must be over-approximated (use ρ = max possible test fraction), leading to looser but still sound bounds

channel_capacity_catalog.description: Two-tier catalog of channel capacity bounds for ML pipeline operations. Tier 1 provides tight bounds with proved tightness factors κ. Tier 2 provides sound generic bounds via Gaussian channel parameterized by observed provenance.

tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: The sample mean X̄ = (1/n)Σ X_i is a sufficient statistic for the population mean under Gaussian model. Treating the n_te test rows as the 'message' and X̄ as the channel output, this is a Gaussian channel with SNR = n_te/n_tr = ρ/(1-ρ). Shannon capacity: ½ log₂(1 + SNR).
tier1_tight_bounds.example: n=10000, n_te=100, ρ=0.01: C ≈ 0.007 bits/feature. n=200, n_te=100, ρ=0.5: C = 0.5 bits/feature.

tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: Sample variance follows a scaled chi-squared distribution. The channel from test rows to s² is more complex than mean (quadratic dependence). Upper bound via the chi-squared channel capacity, which is ½ log₂(1 + 2σ⁴_te/σ⁴_total) normalized by sample size.
Sum = n × mean, so leaks mean information plus the count of test rows contributing. The count term is bounded by log₂(n_te + 1).
Count of elements in a group reveals at most log₂(n_te + 1) bits about which test rows are included.

tier1_tight_bounds.bound: C_median(ρ, n) ≤ log₂(n·ρ + 1) + ½ log₂(πe/2 · n·ρ·(1-ρ))
tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: Reduce to rank channel: the median's rank among test rows reveals which test rows are below/above it. The rank is bounded by n_te, giving log₂(n_te+1) bits. The additional term accounts for the continuous value at that rank position via order-statistic entropy. This is loose for heavy-tailed distributions where the median is poorly constrained.

tier1_tight_bounds.bound: C_quantile(ρ, n, p) ≤ log₂(n·ρ + 1) + H_bin(p) where H_bin is binary entropy
tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: Similar to median but the rank-channel capacity depends on the quantile level p. Extreme quantiles (p near 0 or 1) have lower capacity since fewer test rows are near them.

tier1_tight_bounds.bound: C_min(ρ, n) ≤ ρ · H(X) + (1-ρ) · 0 = ρ · H(X)
tier1_tight_bounds.tightness_factor: κ = O(1) when min/max comes from test data; data-dependent otherwise
tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: If the minimum comes from a test row (probability ≈ ρ), the channel reveals that row's value: leakage ≈ ρ · H(X_min). If from a training row, zero test information is leaked. Worst case: the extremum always comes from test data.

PCA depends on the covariance matrix Σ̂, which has d(d+1)/2 unique entries. Each entry is a bilinear function of the data, treated as an independent channel. The d² scaling makes this bound vacuously large for d > 50; the empirical refinement phase (Phase 3) is essential for PCA-heavy pipelines. Future work: eigenstructure-aware bounds using Wishart distribution theory could tighten by O(d).
tier1_tight_bounds.note: This bound is the weakest in the catalog. For d=100, it gives ~5000 × C_cov_entry — likely vacuous. The paper must acknowledge this and emphasize that PCA triggers empirical refinement.

tier1_tight_bounds.bound: C_groupby_mean(ρ, G) ≤ |G| · C_mean(ρ_g) where ρ_g is the test fraction per group
tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: GroupBy applies the aggregation independently per group. Each group's mean leaks C_mean(ρ_g) bits. Total: sum over groups. Worst case: |G| groups each with ρ_g = ρ. Provenance tracking gives exact ρ_g per group from the dynamic phase.

Target encoding computes E[Y|group] using combined train+test data. The channel capacity from test Y values to the encoded feature is bounded by the conditional entropy H(Y|group), which is the maximum information that can be extracted from target values within each group.
By T1 (decomposition lemma): fit computes (mean, std) → 2 sufficient statistics. C_fit = C_mean + C_std. Transform is pointwise: (x - mean)/std. Total: C_fit(ρ) + b_input.

tier1_tight_bounds.tightness_factor: κ = O(1) to O(log n) depending on data
tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: By T1: fit computes (min, max). Transform is pointwise: (x - min)/(max - min). Looser than StandardScaler because min/max bounds are data-dependent.

OHE fitted on combined data may include categories seen only in test data. The number of test-only categories reveals information about the test set. Each test-only category contributes 1 bit (present/absent). The bound is tight because the category list is exactly the information that leaks.
Imputer with mean strategy computes the column mean (including test rows) and fills missing values. Identical channel to mean aggregation.

Each entry σ̂_ij = (1/n)Σ(x_i - x̄_i)(x_j - x̄_j) is a bilinear function of the data. The channel from test rows to σ̂_ij involves both the direct contribution and the indirect effect through x̄_i and x̄_j. Upper bound via Gaussian channel with quadratic SNR.

tier1_tight_bounds.bound: C_corr(ρ, n) ≤ C_cov_entry(ρ) + C_var(ρ) (via ratio bound)
tier1_tight_bounds.derivation: Pearson correlation r = σ̂_ij / (s_i · s_j). Information through the ratio is bounded by information through numerator plus denominator.

tier2_generic_bounds.formula: C_generic(ρ, n) = ½ log₂(1 + ρ/(1-ρ)) × d_output
tier2_generic_bounds.description: Standard Gaussian channel capacity parameterized by observed ρ, applied per output dimension. This is a sound but potentially loose bound for any operation that mixes train and test rows. It treats each output dimension as an independent Gaussian channel.
applicable_to: feature selection based on combined statistics
any pandas operation not in Tier 1
tier2_generic_bounds.tightness: Sound but may be 10-50× loose for complex operations. Correct severity ordering is maintained.

infinity_bounds.rationale: These operations have data-dependent, non-sufficient-statistic channels. No static bound exists that is tighter than trivial H(D_te). The tool reports 'unknown leakage — conservative ∞ bound' for these stages while providing useful bounds for remaining stages.

A1_dynamic_dag_extraction.name: Dynamic PI-DAG Extraction
A1_dynamic_dag_extraction.input: Python ML pipeline script path, dataset
A1_dynamic_dag_extraction.output: PI-DAG G = (V, E, τ, ρ, σ) serialized as MessagePack
A1_dynamic_dag_extraction.description: Instruments the pipeline execution using sys.settrace for call/return tracking and API-level monkey-patching of pandas (300+ methods) and sklearn (50+ estimators). Detects train_test_split as partition boundary. Propagates provenance via roaring bitmaps through every DataFrame operation.
A1_dynamic_dag_extraction.complexity: O(T_exec + n·d) where T_exec is pipeline execution time and n·d is the provenance bitmap propagation cost
key_steps: 1. Install sys.settrace callback and monkey-patch pandas/sklearn APIs
2. Execute the pipeline; on each intercepted API call, create a DAG node
3. On train_test_split detection, initialize provenance bitmaps (test rows = 1, train rows = 0)
4. For each subsequent operation, propagate provenance bitmaps: merges use bitmap OR/AND per join type; aggregations compute ρ = popcount(test_bitmap) / total_rows; transforms pass through
5. Record (operation_type, source_location, input_columns, output_columns, shapes, ρ) at each node
6. Serialize the completed PI-DAG via MessagePack to the Rust backend
edge_cases: Chained methods: df.groupby('a').transform('mean').fillna(0) → 3 DAG nodes in sequence
ColumnTransformer: parallel branches detected via thread/coroutine tracking
Exception paths: try/except around operations → only successful path recorded in DAG
Memory limit: if provenance bitmap exceeds 500MB for a single merge, fall back to ρ=0.5

A2_abstract_fixpoint.name: Abstract Fixpoint Computation (Worklist Algorithm)
A2_abstract_fixpoint.input: PI-DAG G, channel capacity catalog, initial abstract state σ₀#
A2_abstract_fixpoint.output: Fixpoint abstract state σ# mapping each (node, column) → (origins, bit_bound)
pseudocode: σ# ← σ₀#  // Initialize: source nodes get (∅, 0) or (origins, 0)
W ← topological_sort(G.nodes)  // Worklist in reverse postorder
for each successor u of v in G:
contributing_bounds ← collect bounds from left/right inputs
// Unroll: treat as K-fold repeated pipeline
A2_abstract_fixpoint.complexity: O(K × d × H) = O(K × d × 69) for column-independent ops; O(K × d² × H) for cross-column ops. For typical pipeline (K=50, d=200): ~690,000 lattice updates ≈ <100ms in Rust.
A2_abstract_fixpoint.correctness: Termination: finite lattice height (69) + monotone transfer functions → worklist empties in finite iterations. Soundness: each transfer function F_k is sound by construction from channel capacity bounds (T2). Fixpoint: Tarski's theorem guarantees least fixpoint exists.

A3_channel_capacity_computation.name: Channel Capacity Dispatch
A3_channel_capacity_computation.input: Operation type τ, provenance fraction ρ, sample size n, dimensionality d
A3_channel_capacity_computation.description: Dispatches to Tier 1 tight bound or Tier 2 generic bound based on operation type. Handles numerical edge cases (ρ=0 → C=0, ρ=1 → C=∞, small n → finite-sample corrections).
numerical_stability: For small ρ: use Taylor expansion log₂(1+x) ≈ x/ln(2) to avoid floating-point cancellation
For large d (PCA): cap at B_max=64 bits per feature to prevent overflow
For ρ=0 (train-only data): short-circuit to C=0

A4_mincut_attribution.name: Information-Theoretic Min-Cut Attribution
A4_mincut_attribution.input: PI-DAG G with edge capacities from σ#
A4_mincut_attribution.description: For each output feature j, compute the minimum s-t cut in G where s = test-data source nodes and t = the node producing feature j. Edge capacities are the bit-bounds from the abstract analysis. The min-cut identifies the bottleneck stage(s).
A4_mincut_attribution.algorithm: Standard max-flow/min-cut (Ford-Fulkerson or Dinic's algorithm) on the PI-DAG subgraph relevant to each output feature.
A4_mincut_attribution.complexity: O(V × E) per output feature using Dinic's algorithm. For typical pipelines (V≈50, E≈100): negligible.
A4_mincut_attribution.tie_breaking: When multiple minimum cuts exist, report the cut closest to the source (earliest pipeline stage) as the primary attribution target.

A5_empirical_refinement.name: Optional Empirical Refinement (Phase 3)
A5_empirical_refinement.input: Abstract bounds from Phase 2, observed DataFrame data at each node
A5_empirical_refinement.description: Uses KSG (Kraskov-Stögbauer-Grassberger) k-nearest-neighbor mutual information estimator to compute non-parametric MI estimates. Clamps: final_bound = min(abstract_bound, ksg_estimate + confidence_margin). Confidence margin uses one-sided upper bound at confidence level 1-α (default α=0.05).
A5_empirical_refinement.when_to_trigger: Opt-in; recommended when abstract bounds exceed a user-configurable threshold (default: 10 bits per feature)
A5_empirical_refinement.soundness_note: The clamped bound is sound at confidence level 1-α. The abstract bound alone is sound unconditionally (given the soundness conditions). This phase trades unconditional soundness for tighter bounds at a specified confidence level.

end_to_end.phase1_dag_extraction: O(T_exec + n·d) — dominated by pipeline execution time T_exec
end_to_end.phase2_abstract_analysis: O(K × d² × H) worst case, O(K × d × H) typical — under 1 second in Rust for K=50, d=200
end_to_end.phase3_empirical_refinement: O(n² × d) for KSG estimation — optional, 1-10 seconds depending on dataset size
end_to_end.phase4_attribution: O(d × V × E) — negligible for typical DAGs
end_to_end.total_analysis_time: Under 10 seconds median on laptop CPU (excluding pipeline execution)
end_to_end.memory: O(K × d) for abstract states + O(n / 64) per provenance bitmap (roaring bitmap compression)

scalability_limits.max_features: d ≈ 1000 before cross-column operations (PCA) make analysis slow (d² factor)
scalability_limits.max_pipeline_stages: K ≈ 500 before worklist iterations become noticeable
scalability_limits.max_rows_for_provenance: n ≈ 10M before roaring bitmaps consume significant memory (>1GB for pathological merges)

language_split.python: ~15K LoC — CLI, instrumentation, DAG extraction, PyO3 bindings
language_split.rust: ~40K LoC — abstract domain, fixpoint engine, channel catalog, attribution, SARIF generation

module_mapping.D1_pidag: rust crate `taintflow-dag` — DAG data structure, MessagePack deserialization
module_mapping.D2_lattice: rust crate `taintflow-lattice` — partition-taint lattice, join/meet/compare operations
module_mapping.D3_abstract_domain: rust crate `taintflow-domain` — DataFrame abstract domain, column maps
module_mapping.D4_transfer_functions: rust crate `taintflow-transfer` — 80 pandas + 50 sklearn transfer function implementations
module_mapping.A2_worklist: rust crate `taintflow-engine` — worklist algorithm, RPO ordering, Rayon parallelism
module_mapping.A3_capacity: rust crate `taintflow-capacity` — channel capacity formulas, numerical implementations
module_mapping.A4_attribution: rust crate `taintflow-attribution` — min-cut, SARIF output, terminal reports
module_mapping.A1_instrumentation: python package `taintflow.instrument` — sys.settrace, monkey-patches, provenance bitmaps

theorem_to_module_mapping.T1_decomposition: taintflow-transfer: FitTransformHandler trait implementation for each covered estimator
theorem_to_module_mapping.T2_soundness: taintflow-engine: the worklist loop IS the soundness proof's constructive content
theorem_to_module_mapping.T5_termination: taintflow-engine: lattice height check as assertion in debug builds

critical_path: Month 2-5: Fit-transform lemma proof + channel catalog derivations
Month 3-6: Transfer functions for 50 sklearn + 80 pandas ops

hypotheses.statement: TaintFlow bounds are sound: reported ≥ true MI for all analyzed operations
hypotheses.metric: fraction of (operation, pipeline) pairs where bound ≥ true MI
hypotheses.target: 1.00 on synthetic, ≥0.99 on real (allowing instrumentation gaps)

hypotheses.statement: Tier 1 bounds are tight: median bound/truth ratio ≤ 5×
hypotheses.metric: median ratio across Tier 1 operations on synthetic pipelines

hypotheses.statement: Tier 2 bounds are useful: median bound/truth ratio ≤ 20×
hypotheses.metric: median ratio across Tier 2 operations on synthetic pipelines

hypotheses.statement: Detection recall ≥ 0.95 on Yang et al. corpus

hypotheses.metric: rank correlation between reported and empirical leakage
wall-clock time for Phase 2 analysis, median over corpus

hypotheses.statement: Coverage ≥ 95% of real pipelines produce non-∞ bounds on ≥1 feature
hypotheses.metric: fraction of pipelines with at least one finite bound

benchmarks.synthetic: 500+ parameterized pipelines, 30 leakage patterns, ρ ∈ {0, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5}, linear-Gaussian subset for gold-standard tightness
benchmarks.real_world: 200+ Kaggle kernels from Yang et al. (ASE 2022) with manual leakage labels + 100 leakage-free controls

evaluation_plan.statistical_methodology: 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap; McNemar's test for detection; Wilcoxon signed-rank for continuous; Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons

fatal_risks.impact: Crown jewel covers fewer estimators. Fallback: prove for StandardScaler/PCA/SimpleImputer only (still novel and useful). Remaining estimators get conservative bounds.
fatal_risks.mitigation: Prove for core set first. If general theorem fails, the paper presents 'decomposition for sufficient-statistic estimators' — smaller but still publishable.

fatal_risks.risk: d² PCA bound is vacuous for d > 50
fatal_risks.impact: PCA-heavy pipelines get useless abstract bounds. Severity ordering still works (PCA correctly flagged as high-leakage).
fatal_risks.mitigation: Emphasize empirical refinement for PCA. Acknowledge as honest limitation. Future work: eigenstructure-aware bounds.

fatal_risks.risk: Conditional soundness perceived as weak by reviewers
fatal_risks.impact: Reviewer writes: 'This is dynamic analysis with extra steps.'
fatal_risks.mitigation: Frame as the correct claim for the use case. Emphasize quantitative leap (bits, not binary). Target NeurIPS/ICML systems track where practical impact matters.

serious_risks.mitigation: Two-tier design is robust. Paper presents 'tight bounds for 15-20 common operations, sound bounds for the rest.'

serious_risks.risk: KSG estimator bias for high-dimensional features
serious_risks.mitigation: KSG is opt-in Phase 3. Soundness of Phase 2 does not depend on KSG. Use one-sided confidence bound.

honest_limitations: Requires pipeline execution — cannot audit third-party code without data access
Conditional soundness — bounds hold for observed execution path only
PCA/cross-column bounds scale as d², becoming vacuous for d > 50
pandas API version maintenance is an ongoing engineering burden

genuinely_new: Fit-transform channel decomposition lemma (T1) — no prior QIF work handles this feedback pattern
Partition-taint lattice with provenance-parameterized capacity bounds — first quantitative information-flow abstract domain for DataFrame operations
Systematic channel capacity catalog for ML preprocessing operations — first systematic derivation with tightness factors
The entire approach: hybrid dynamic-static quantitative information-flow analysis for ML pipelines
novel_adaptation: Conditional soundness for quantitative bounds (adapts concolic testing ideas to QIF)
Min-cut attribution applied to ML pipeline DAGs
Sensitivity functions as compositional backbone (adapts DP composition to info-flow)
